title: 1-Board Cedar Desk Accessory Set
summary: A pencil cup, business card holder, mini crate, and paper clip tray — all four cut from a single cedar fence picket.
description:
Every piece here comes from one 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6' cedar fence picket, which makes this the kind of project you can finish an entire desk-organizer set from a single offcut. The trade-off for that efficiency is that the cut list reads almost like a woodworking puzzle: pieces get roughed to an oversized length first, then trimmed down to their final size in a second pass, so read each cut-list line as two steps, not one.

Because every part is small and the stock is thin, a stop block on your miter saw is worth setting up before you start — it makes repeat cuts (there are a lot of them, especially on the mini crate's six side slats) far more consistent than eyeballing a tape measure each time. Glue does more of the work than nails on parts this small; use the nails mainly to hold pieces in place while the glue sets, not as the primary joint.

tools.note: A stop block pays off here — many of the cuts repeat at the same short length.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft
materials.note: The entire set comes from this one board.

materials.name: Finishing supplies
materials.note: Whatever finish you'd like — clear coat lets the cedar grain and color show.

steps.title: Build the pencil cup
steps.body: Cut two front/back pieces 3-1/2" square, two sides 3-1/2" long by 1-5/8" wide, and a bottom 3-1/2" x 2-5/8". The two 3-1/2" front/back pieces run the full width with the 1-5/8" sides fitted between them, which is exactly why the bottom is 2-5/8" — 1-5/8" plus 1/2" of picket at each end. Glue and tack the four walls into a box 3-1/2" x 2-5/8" and 3-1/2" tall, then glue and nail the bottom on underneath so it caps the walls flush. Sand the top rim and break its inside and outside edges; that rim is what your hand touches every time you reach for a pencil.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

steps.title: Build the paper clip tray
steps.body: Cut two front/back pieces 4" long and two sides 3" long, all 1-1/2" wide, plus a 3" square bottom. The 4" front and back run the full length with the 3" sides between them, giving a tray 4" square outside and 3" square inside — which is precisely what the 3" bottom drops into. Glue and pin the four walls first, check both diagonals, then set the bottom flush with the bottom edges. At only 1-1/2" deep a tray shows racking immediately, so get it square before the glue grabs.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

steps.title: Build the business card holder
steps.body: Cut all three pieces 4" long: a back 2-1/4" wide, a bottom 1" wide and a front lip 1-1/4" wide. Glue and pin the bottom to the back so the back leans back rather than standing plumb — cards then rest against it and stay readable instead of tipping forward out of the slot. Add the 1-1/4" front lip along the front edge of the bottom, leaving the bottom's own 1/2" thickness as the floor of the slot. At 4" long it takes a standard 3-1/2" business card with room to get a fingertip in alongside.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

steps.title: Build the mini desktop crate
steps.body: Cut three 3-1/2" squares and six slats 4-1/2" long by 1" wide. Two of the squares are the crate ends and the slats run across their outside faces, making the crate 4-1/2" long — which leaves exactly 3-1/2" between the ends, so the third square is the floor and drops straight in. Three slats per side on a 3-1/2" end cover 3", leaving two 1/4" gaps to lay out; the gaps are the whole point of a crate, so mark them rather than eyeballing. Sand every piece in all four projects, break the edges, and apply your finish.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

images.alt: Cedar desk accessory set including a pencil cup, business card holder, mini crate, and paper clip tray
